avoid: Large labeled controls for refresh, retry, view output, open source, or similar inspection utilities.
Icon-only treatment for unfamiliar, destructive, or primary actions.
checks: Are routine utilities compact without competing with the primary action?
Does every icon-only action have an accessible name and concise tooltip?
Would a first-time user understand any action that remains unlabeled?
exceptions: The action is primary, destructive, unfamiliar, or difficult to represent precisely. Use a concise labeled control and the appropriate confirmation or recovery treatment. Clarity and safety outrank density.
not_when: The action is unfamiliar, destructive, or the primary transition.
The icon cannot express the action precisely.
prefer: Use a semantically exact icon, accessible name, and concise tooltip.
Use a labeled button for the one action that advances the primary workflow.
statement: Routine inspection and operational utilities default to compact icon-only controls with concise tooltips and accessible names; reserve labeled buttons for primary workflow transitions.
title: Keep routine utilities compact
when: The action is routine, local, reversible, and repeatedly used in a dense surface.

avoid: Explanatory labels, borders, sections, and gaps that do not change a decision.
Oversized or uniformly heavy chrome.
checks: Can any label, border, section, or gap disappear without reducing understanding?
Does visual weight match product importance?
Is content still the dominant surface?
not_when: Removing the element would hide state, orientation, recovery, or an otherwise undiscoverable action.
prefer: Let content and the current job establish hierarchy.
Use the smallest amount of persistent structure that preserves orientation and state.
statement: Every label, border, section, menu width, and vertical gap must earn its visual weight; prefer compact, content-led layouts over explanatory chrome.
title: Make every piece of chrome earn its weight
when: Several persistent UI elements compete with the content or primary job.

avoid: Custom components whose anatomy or behavior feels unrelated to the host product.
Copying surface color while inventing a different interaction language.
checks: What existing surface is the closest behavioral sibling?
Which real tokens and components are reused?
If a new primitive is necessary, is the reason explicit?
not_when: Existing primitives cannot express the capability without creating a worse mental model.
prefer: Find the closest product-native sibling and reuse its structure, tokens, and behavior.
Introduce a new primitive only when existing ones cannot express the job.
statement: Start from the closest established product surface, component, behavior, and token set; extend it before introducing bespoke UI.
title: Extend the existing product language
when: A new or changed surface has an established product neighbor or primitive.

avoid: Generic system chrome.
Abstract mockups that cannot be recognized as the intended product object.
checks: Can the intended product object be recognized without explanation?
Does the surface feel native beside its closest host-product sibling?
Is the anatomy credible at realistic content density?
not_when: A deliberately brandless technical wireframe is the requested artifact, while still retaining recognizable anatomy.
prefer: Use realistic product anatomy and density.
Ground the surface in host-product components and tokens.
statement: New product surfaces need credible anatomy, density, and host-product fidelity; do not ship generic system UI or mock-like bespoke compositions.
title: Make new surfaces feel product-native and real
when: A design will represent or become a real product surface.

avoid: Showing an empty card or zero-count section merely because the container exists.
Leaving a visible empty surface without orientation or its first useful action.
checks: Does the empty container help the user act or understand state?
If not, can it collapse entirely?
If guidance remains, is it compact and actionable?
prefer: Collapse irrelevant empty containers so no artificial empty state remains.
When the surface remains, explain what belongs there and keep the first useful action compact.
statement: Treat emptiness first as a structural decision: remove or collapse a section with no useful next action; when the surface must remain, explain what belongs there and provide the first useful action in a compact empty state.
title: Treat empty state as semantic absence
when: A proposed or existing surface has no items or content, requiring a decision whether the surface should remain visible.

avoid: Replacing editable choices with a non-editable visual artifact.
checks: Can the user edit the actual decision at its canonical source?
Is any HTML block scoped to one visual job?
not_when: The requested deliverable is primarily a visual or interactive demonstration.
prefer: Keep the decision itself in editable, commentable source.
Use scoped HTML only where visual shape or interaction materially improves understanding.
statement: For durable decisions in a note, default to editable Markdown tables or checklists; use HTML for a focused visual or interaction demonstration rather than as an opaque replacement.
title: Keep durable decisions editable at the source
when: The artifact captures decisions the user will revise or comment on.

avoid: Childish saturation.
Making every state the same neutral or accent color.
checks: Does each color difference carry meaning?
Does the palette feel product-native and professional?
Are distinct states still distinguishable?
not_when: A deliberately neutral or brandless wireframe is the artifact.
prefer: Use the product token system and reserve color differences for meaningful semantics.
Balance professional restraint with enough variety to distinguish states.
statement: Use product-native tokenized color with tasteful semantic variety; avoid both childish saturation and blanket desaturation or one-accent monotony.
title: Use restrained semantic color variety
when: Color carries state or status meaning in product UI.

avoid: Adding hover or edit controls that push content or resize the row.
checks: Does geometry remain stable when controls appear?
Can the pointer target stay under the pointer?
Is intentional expansion visually anchored?
not_when: Intentional content expansion is the action and the anchor remains stable.
prefer: Overlay transient actions or reserve their space.
Keep the anchor and neighboring content stable.
statement: Hover, drag, and edit affordances must not shift row or panel geometry; transient controls should overlay or use reserved space.
title: Reveal controls without reflow
when: A transient affordance appears inside an existing row or panel.

avoid: Fixed cramped surfaces for variable content.
Changing a neighboring minimum merely to fit one state.
checks: Is there a useful growth range and explicit maximum?
Are unrelated constraints preserved?
Does the narrowest supported viewport remain usable?
not_when: Growth would exceed a viewport or destabilize the primary workspace.
prefer: Grow with useful content until a clear maximum.
Preserve unrelated minimum sizes and narrow-viewport constraints.
statement: Let inputs and surfaces grow with useful content to an explicit maximum; do not alter unrelated minimum constraints.
title: Let content grow within explicit bounds
when: Content length or user editing meaningfully changes the useful footprint.

avoid: Duplicate seams.
Turning a structural divider into the only drag-state indicator.
checks: Does the divider explain stable structure?
Are adjacent seams duplicated?
Is transient state communicated independently?
not_when: The boundary is an interactive resizable splitter with clear affordance.
prefer: Use one quiet consistent boundary where stable grouping needs it.
Use a dedicated drag or selection affordance for transient state.
statement: Dividers communicate stable grouping, not transient drag state; avoid duplicate seams and keep related boundaries visually consistent.
title: Use dividers to express stable structure
when: A visible line separates persistent regions or groups.

avoid: Opening a navigation surface because the pointer merely crossed it.
checks: Can pointer movement accidentally open or navigate?
Is keyboard activation equivalent?
not_when: A platform-native pattern requires hover disclosure and is safely reversible.
prefer: Require a click or equivalent explicit activation to open navigation.
Keep hover effects local and non-navigational.
statement: Navigation menus should open on explicit click rather than incidental hover; hover may reveal secondary local row controls.
title: Open navigation on explicit intent
when: A menu or navigation surface changes place or reveals a substantial panel.

avoid: Navigating to a detached copy, placeholder, or unreachable location.
checks: Does activation reveal and focus the real source object?
Is the destination recoverable when the source is missing?
not_when: The source object no longer exists and the UI explicitly presents a historical record.
prefer: Anchor navigation to the real editable or inspectable source object.
statement: A list item representing in-document content must navigate to the live anchored object rather than an unreachable surrogate.
title: Navigate to the live source object
when: A secondary list represents content that exists in a primary document or canvas.

avoid: Independent layouts, status vocabularies, and row structures for sibling resource types.
checks: Do sibling resource pages share the same structural grammar?
Are differences explained by real resource semantics?
Can one shared component own the common behavior?
not_when: A resource has a fundamentally different user job or lifecycle.
prefer: Reuse the same information skeleton and shared components.
Express only genuine type-specific differences.
statement: Related resource types should share a layout, taxonomy, and component spine, varying only where type, ownership, or state creates a real difference.
title: Give related resources one shared spine
when: Several resource types live in one product hub or lifecycle.

avoid: Mixing capability, business model, market, and context in one filter.
checks: Do all options answer the same question?
Would any option fit equally in another facet?
Can cross-cutting terms remain searchable without becoming filters?
not_when: A small flat set has no meaningful facets.
prefer: Give each visible facet one mutually understandable classification dimension.
Keep useful cross-cutting terms searchable as metadata.
statement: Visible filters must represent comparable dimensions; mixed capability, market, and business-model terms belong in searchable metadata rather than one facet.
title: Make each visible facet one coherent dimension
when: Users browse or filter a typed collection.

avoid: Giving every section the same weight because they share a component family.
checks: Can a skimmer tell which section owns the primary job?
Are peers equal and non-peers differentiated?
Does hierarchy remain within the shared system?
not_when: The sections are true peers that users compare directly.
prefer: Use hierarchy to distinguish primary work, supporting context, and metadata.
Keep sibling styling consistent without erasing role differences.
statement: Sibling surfaces with different jobs need visibly different hierarchy; sections should not merely look equally flat.
title: Make hierarchy reflect the job
when: Adjacent sections serve different user jobs or importance levels.

avoid: Changing an entire card when only one row or action is interactive.
checks: Does the hover region match the actual click target?
Does unrelated content remain visually stable?
not_when: The entire card is one interactive target.
prefer: Apply hover feedback to the smallest owning interactive region.
statement: Hover styling and action changes belong to the owning row or control rather than an unrelated whole card.
title: Keep hover effects local
when: A nested action or row needs hover feedback.

avoid: Inert controls that look actionable.
Pressed styling disconnected from actual state.
checks: Can every visible control be activated as expected?
Does visual state match application state?
Is unavailable behavior clearly disabled or removed?
not_when: A clearly labeled static demonstration is intentionally non-interactive.
prefer: Wire every visible control to real behavior.
Reflect real selected, pressed, loading, and disabled state.
statement: A visible control must work and its pressed or selected appearance must reflect real state; otherwise remove, disable, or fix it.
title: Make controls behaviorally honest
when: A surface presents an interactive control or stateful action.

avoid: Repeating the same state label throughout a row.
Adding a second toggle that competes with the primary state control.
checks: Where is the canonical state shown?
Does the owning control reveal the next valid action?
Is the same status repeated without adding information?
not_when: A separate status is needed for monitoring at a different scope.
prefer: Let the owning control communicate current state and next action.
Keep one canonical status location.
statement: Encode installed, running, paused, and similar state once as a distinct state of the owning control; do not repeat it across rows or add competing toggles.
title: Represent state once on the owning control
when: A resource has a persistent state and a related action.

avoid: Using a drag handle as an unrelated click target.
checks: Does the target have one predictable gesture meaning?
Are keyboard and touch alternatives available?
Can an accidental activation be undone?
not_when: A platform convention explicitly combines equivalent gestures without ambiguity.
prefer: Assign one primary intent to each gesture target.
Expose separate actions through separate controls.
statement: A drag handle drags; do not overload it with click-to-group, copy, or unrelated actions.
title: Give each gesture one intent
when: A control supports drag or another continuous gesture.

avoid: Decorative emoji in product chrome.
Ambiguous icons that erase essential state or quantity.
checks: Is the icon semantically exact?
Does removing text hide quantity or state?
Is the control still accessible without hover?
not_when: The concept is unfamiliar or has no precise established symbol.
prefer: Use a canonical symbol with an accessible name.
Retain counts or state when they change the user's decision.
statement: Replace text with an icon only when the symbol is semantically exact; retain essential quantity or state and avoid decorative emoji.
title: Use icons only when they preserve meaning
when: Text is being replaced to reduce density.

avoid: Implementation terms that do not change the user's action.
Granular process detail in primary copy.
checks: Does the copy lead with the user capability?
Can internal terms be removed without losing meaning?
Is the observable outcome clear?
not_when: The audience explicitly needs technical implementation detail.
prefer: Name the user job, capability, and observable outcome.
Use product vocabulary consistently.
statement: Write for the user's task and product vocabulary rather than internal implementation; avoid overly technical or granular explanations.
title: Write in user-capability language
when: Copy explains a feature, action, or result to the user.

avoid: Describing click behavior when the feature uses hover, or vice versa.
Copy based only on intended behavior.
checks: Does the described gesture match the current rendered product?
Does the stated outcome occur?
Was the interaction verified directly?
not_when: The text is explicitly conceptual and labeled as such.
prefer: Verify and name the exact shipped gesture and result.
statement: UI copy must name the real gesture and outcome, especially hover versus click, rather than describe an idealized interaction.
title: Describe the interaction that actually ships
when: Copy instructs the user how to interact with a feature.

avoid: Grouping by implementation module when the user jobs differ.
Splitting one job into several arbitrary feature fragments.
checks: Can each group be named as one user job?
Do controls in the group share an outcome?
Are distinct capabilities separately legible?
not_when: Side-by-side comparison of true peer capabilities is the job.
prefer: Group controls, examples, and explanation around one coherent user job.
Separate capabilities when they have different goals or outcomes.
statement: Combine controls and explanations that serve one user job; split genuinely separate capabilities into separately legible features.
title: Group content by the user's job
when: A page or document describes multiple controls or capabilities.

avoid: Repeating labels or status already conveyed by the owning control or section.
checks: Does each metadata item change a decision?
Is the same status shown elsewhere?
Can a label disappear without ambiguity?
not_when: Redundancy is required for accessibility, safety, or cross-context orientation.
prefer: Show metadata that changes interpretation or the next action.
Choose one canonical location for each status.
statement: Do not repeat status, labels, or explanatory metadata already clear from the surrounding object; show only what changes a decision.
title: Remove redundant metadata
when: A dense surface repeats descriptive or status text.

avoid: Copying an item into a detached composer.
Moving the item because it entered edit mode.
checks: Is the edited object still the same object?
Does it remain in place?
Does commit update that exact object?
not_when: The product explicitly creates a new derived object and communicates that transition.
prefer: Edit and commit the same object at the same location.
Preserve ordering unless reordering is the explicit action.
statement: Editing an item in place preserves its position and commits back to that same item rather than moving the work into a separate composer or reordered draft.
title: Preserve object identity during in-place editing
when: The user invokes edit on an existing object.

avoid: Opening an editor without focus.
Leaving selection or hover state that visually competes with editing.
checks: Does focus land on the intended input?
Can the user still identify the source object?
Are competing transient states suppressed?
not_when: The edit requires a deliberate multi-step modal workflow for safety.
prefer: Move focus immediately to the real editing target.
Keep the source anchor visible and quiet competing hover state.
statement: Direct edit should focus the intended object immediately, make its anchor obvious, and suppress transient hover or selection interference.
title: Focus direct editing on the real anchor
when: A user invokes editing directly from an object.

avoid: Fixing each component with unrelated literal values.
checks: Can each visual value be named by semantic role?
Do related components reuse the same ladder?
Would one token change update the family coherently?
not_when: A one-off visual artifact intentionally has no product-system role.
prefer: Define a small semantic set of surface, border, spacing, and elevation roles.
Reuse roles across related components.
statement: Use a small semantic tokenized surface system rather than ad hoc spacing and color fixes so related UI stays uniform and maintainable.
title: Use a small semantic token ladder
when: Several related surfaces need visual tuning.

avoid: Completely invisible hit areas.
Hover-only access with no keyboard path.
checks: Can a user locate the affordance before hovering precisely?
Does keyboard focus reveal and operate it?
Is the tooltip supplemental rather than essential?
not_when: The control is always visible or the entire containing object is the target.
prefer: Leave a quiet positional cue at rest.
Reveal the control on hover and focus with an accessible name and concise tooltip.
statement: When a control appears on hover, show a quiet at-rest cue for its location and support keyboard focus plus a tooltip; users must not memorize invisible hit areas.
title: Make the locus of hover controls discoverable
when: A control is hidden until hover to reduce density.

avoid: Independent context models for composition and history.
checks: Does the same context retain identity across surfaces?
Are compression and expansion rules consistent?
Can the user tell what was actually sent?
not_when: A surface intentionally represents a different immutable historical snapshot.
prefer: Use the same ordering, summary, count, and expansion logic wherever agent context appears.
statement: Context pills, images, and threads should compress and expand through one coherent model across the composer and action timeline.
title: Use one coherent model for agent context
when: The same agent context appears in more than one lifecycle surface.

avoid: Unmounting or disabling the docked composer during adjacent navigation or resize.
checks: Does the composer survive resize and navigation?
Is draft context preserved?
Can the user still submit or recover?
not_when: The user closes or explicitly undocks it, or a hard permission boundary requires dismissal.
prefer: Preserve composer presence, content, and operability across normal workspace transitions.
statement: A docked composer remains present and operable through resize, panel, note, and timeline transitions.
title: Keep a docked agent composer persistent
when: The user has explicitly docked a composer for sustained work.

avoid: Declaring UI correct because code looks correct.
Using stale screenshots or an unverified server.
checks: Was the current rendered target inspected directly?
Were the relevant state and viewport exercised?
Does the report distinguish observed behavior from inference?
not_when: The task is explicitly source-only and visual state is out of scope, which must be stated.
prefer: Inspect the current rendered target in the relevant state and viewport.
Report what was observed separately from what source implies.
statement: An agent must not infer visible UI correctness from source text alone; it needs direct inspection of the rendered target before explaining or claiming success.
title: Inspect rendered state before claiming visual correctness
when: An agent explains, reviews, or claims completion of visible UI work.

avoid: Fake or placeholder product UI.
Stale, low-resolution, cropped, or cramped captures that hide the behavior.
checks: Does the artifact come from the current product state?
Are inputs realistic and safe to publish?
Does framing demonstrate the intended user job?
Was final resolution inspected?
not_when: A rough conceptual sketch is explicitly the requested stage and is labeled conceptual.
prefer: Use current product UI with realistic safe content.
Frame the state and viewport around the user job.
Inspect final-resolution assets.
statement: Design evidence must use the current product UI and realistic inputs, framed to demonstrate the user job; reject fake, stale, low-resolution, or cramped captures.
title: Demonstrate work with current realistic evidence
when: A visual artifact is used to approve, document, or claim a design outcome.

avoid: Bespoke scenes that look like generic diagrams instead of usable interface patterns.
Duplicating component anatomy separately for each reference.
checks: Does the reference use reusable UI anatomy rather than a bespoke scene?
Is the represented pattern recognizable at gallery and inspector scale?
Do screenshots confirm the composition remains readable in its target surface?
not_when: The requested artifact is a deliberately abstract diagram rather than a UI reference.
prefer: Build reference compositions from reusable controls, containers, and layout primitives.
Preserve recognizable interface anatomy while keeping the visual treatment intentionally neutral.
statement: For Pattern Atlas references, compose recognizable UI anatomy from reusable components rather than drawing bespoke abstract scenes.
title: Compose pattern references from reusable UI anatomy
when: A Pattern Atlas reference must represent a reusable product pattern.
